notes.title: The scar of friendship will never be healed.
notes.source: William and Marguerite Sears, "Black Sunlight," Bahá’í World, Vol. 12, pp. 926-927
notes.whySelected: The Kampala incident won because it was short, complete, unusually literary, and emotionally memorable: a young Buganda moves from bewilderment to a heart-deep friendship with Bahá’u’lláh. It accepted a Bahá’í World repetition penalty because the protagonist, African setting, and understanding/friendship register were fresh against recent ‘Abdu’l-Bahá danger, service, martyrdom, and teaching-method lanes.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Bahá’í World item, William Sears/Africa travel reminiscence, Kampala/Uganda House of Worship image, simple-conversion/understanding vignette, or 'friendship of the heart' quote. Prefer a non-Bahá’í World source with women/community transformation, Promulgation only if concrete and story-shaped, or an authorized memoir outside recent source families.

notes.title: Please God this dynamite will not explode.
notes.source: Shoghi Effendi, God Passes By, chapter XVII
notes.whySelected: The passage answered the Covenant/providence target with a complete, dramatic arc: the second commission arrives in ‘Akká, the ship appears ready to carry ‘Abdu’l-Bahá away, then turns toward Constantinople and the danger is providentially averted. It beat Shamsu’d-Duhá from Memorials because that runner-up, while vivid, substantially overlapped the existing Ṭáhirih Memorials entry and had weaker direct image fit.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another ‘Abdu’l-Bahá imprisonment-in-‘Akká passage, ship/deportation providence scene, Covenant-breaker crisis from God Passes By chapter XVII, or Acre Prison image. Prefer a non-‘Abdu’l-Bahá protagonist, women/community transformation outside Memorials overlap, or an authorized memoir with a fresh emotional register.

notes.title: Go back and sow the seeds.
notes.source: Reginald Grant Barrow, Mother's Stories, p. 39
notes.whySelected: User-directed featured addition. The anecdote is compact and memorable: ‘Abdu’l-Bahá corrects a discouraged teacher by separating faithful seed-sowing from the desire to count declarations. It uses an existing trusted Mother’s Stories source family and fits the Teaching theme cleanly.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Mother’s Stories p. 39 item, India teaching anecdote, sowing/seed metaphor, or ‘Abdu’l-Bahá instruction-about-methods vignette. Prefer a different source family and a fuller narrative arc next.

notes.title: That fearless maid alone.
notes.whySelected: Zaynab of Zanján won despite a Dawn-Breakers/Bábí repetition penalty because it delivered an exceptional, self-contained women’s courage story outside Ṭáhirih and outside recent Western pioneer/service lanes. It beat Bowery Mission/Promulgation material because those passages were discourse-heavy and ‘Abdu’l-Bahá-service adjacent, and it beat modern Mona-related newspaper/World Order material because those were more source-fragmentary and martyrdom-pressure heavy.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Dawn-Breakers siege, early Bábí martyrdom, Zanján/Ḥujjat passage, women-in-combat story, or courage-under-fire arc. Prefer Covenant steadfastness if rights-safe, concrete community transformation outside Star of the West and Bahá’í World, or a quieter non-‘Abdu’l-Bahá source with a fresh emotional register.

notes.title: Barely fifteen dollars in Australia.
notes.source: Martha Root, "How the Bahá’í Message Came to Australia and New Zealand," Star of the West, Vol. 15, Issue 11, pp. 334-336
notes.whySelected: It answered the requested rotation away from wartime Haifa, courier sacrifice, and Bahá’í World/service pressure with a fresh community-awakening arc: Hyde and Clara Dunn answer ‘Abdu’l-Bahá’s Tablets, arrive in Australia with almost no money, live the life, help establish centers, hold an astonishing South Australia Feast, and carry the same awakening into New Zealand. It beat Agnes Alexander's Tokyo earthquake account because the latter was disaster/service-adjacent and less complete as a spiritual founding story.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Star of the West pioneer travel report, Hyde/Clara Dunn item, Australia/New Zealand origin story, Martha Root article, or 'arrive with little money and establish a community' arc. Prefer women/equality outside early Western pioneers, Covenant steadfastness if rights-safe, a concrete Promulgation action scene, or non-Western teaching/community transformation from a different source family.

notes.title: Haji Ramadan walks through the war.
notes.source: The Chosen Highway, ‘Abdu’l-Bahá in War-time, pp. 206-208
notes.whySelected: It used an underrepresented book-length memoir source and delivered a complete wartime mission arc: Haji Ramadan, old and nearly blind, volunteers for an impossible journey, reaches Tihran, returns with letters and gold intact, and then disappears on a later mission. It beat the Star of the West Garden of Healing article because Haji Ramadan had sharper narrative stakes, cleaner completion, stronger memorability, and a more direct archival image fit.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another ‘Abdu’l-Bahá wartime-Haifa story, messenger-carrying-a-letter account, elderly heroic-sacrifice vignette, Mount Carmel/Haifa image, or Chosen Highway famine/service passage. Prefer women/equality outside Ṭáhirih, community transformation outside wartime Palestine, Covenant steadfastness if rights-safe, or a fresh non-Western teaching story.

notes.title: The season of spring and wine has arrived.
notes.source: A Traveler’s Narrative, part 2, ‘Abdu’l-Bahá’s account of Siyyid Yaḥyá of Daráb and Mullá Muḥammad-‘Alí of Zanján
notes.whySelected: It refreshed the source mix with an underused authoritative book and a concise transformation arc: two learned investigators encounter the Báb’s writings and become public heralds. It beat the Dawn-Breakers moonlit Mákú scene because that runner-up echoed recent Báb/Dawn-Breakers material despite stronger atmosphere.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another early Bábí recognition story, another Vahíd/Siyyid Yaḥyá item, another Shíráz investigation passage, or another short conversion vignette. Prefer a fuller story of women/equality, Covenant steadfastness, community transformation, or service outside Bahá’í World.

notes.title: Exactly what He owed him.
notes.source: The Priceless Pearl, p. 33, Rúḥíyyih Khánum's Egypt memories from Shoghi Effendi
notes.whySelected: The underused book source produced a compact, memorable scene: ‘Abdu’l-Bahá's calm integrity before an abusive coachman, framed by Shoghi Effendi's own recollection. It beat the stronger-but-longer Dawn-Breakers vision account because it refreshed the source mix and finished cleanly in one complete paragraph.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another ‘Abdu’l-Bahá and Shoghi Effendi vignette, another brief single-paragraph reminiscence, or a return to the already-heavy service/Bahá’í World lane unless the story is exceptional.
